coredata.dc:title: Formulation of double nanoemulsions based on pH-sensitive carboxymethyl cellulose /Starch copper doped carbon quantum dots for quercetin controlled release
coredata.dc:creator: Najafi, Mohammad
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Abdous, Majid
Rahdar, Abbas
Pandey, Sadanand
coredata.dc:description: Nanocomposite Hydrogels are versatile and adaptable in drug delivery, suitable for various administrations. They offer prolonged and regulated drug release which makes them valuable in targeting cancer treatments. This study used the water/oil/water approach to construct a drug nanocarrier. It encapsulated hazelnut oil, serving as a protective and organic membrane that enveloped the nanocarrier and regulated drug release. Quercetin (QC), a potent flavonoid with potential therapeutic applications, faces challenges due to its hydrophobic nature and poor solubility. This approach presents a novel approach to enhance QC's solubility and stability by integrating it with copper carbon quantum dots (Cu-CQDs) into a pH-sensitive carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and starch-based hydrogel nanocomposite. This system aims to improve QC delivery while minimizing adverse effects. The nanocomposite exhibited exceptional drug loading and encapsulation efficiencies of 47.00 % ± 0.45 and 86.25 % ± 0.75, respectively, ranking among the top-reported values for similar systems. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to examine molecular interactions. X-ray diffraction (XRD) assessment demonstrated the presence of components beneficial for drug solubility enhancement at 2Θ = 17–24°, where QC's crystalline peaks are absent from the CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC XRD pattern. A particle size of 151.57 ± 37 nm is optimized for selectively targeting cancer cells, minimizing interaction with healthy cells, as revealed through DLS assessment. Morphological characteristics analyzed by FE-SEM support this observation as well. Zeta potential measurement (+38.8 mV) highlights nanocarrier stability. CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC displayed remarkable cytotoxicity against cancerous cells, reducing U87-MG cell viability to 48 % while maintaining high cell viability (92 %) for normal L929 cells. These results emphasize the promise of this nanocomposite as a QC drug delivery system by indicating improved anticancer efficacy with fewer adverse effects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Starch
Carboxymethyl cellulose
Cu Carbon quantum dots
Quercetin
pH-sensitive nancarrier
Controlled release
Brain cancer treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Loading and encapsulation of drug determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Abbas Rahdar thanks from University of Zabol for funding (UOZ-GR-8906). Events, Happy NOWRUZ 1403, Persian New Year of Languages Around the World (1403/01/03, AmirMoez, Zahedan), The authors (Sadanand Pandey) express profound gratitude to Shoolini University for their invaluable support, which played a pivotal role in facilitating the completion of work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of Starch, CMC, CMC/Starch, CQDs, Cu-CQDs, CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs, and CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC nanocarrier.
The XRD patterns of Starch, CMC, CMC/Starch, CQDs, Cu-CQDs, CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs, and CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC nanocarrier.
FESEM micrographs of freeze-dried CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC at varying magnifications (A and B). Zeta potential (C) and Size distribution (D) and of the nanocarrier.
Cumulative release curve for QC in acidic and neutral environments.
QC release fitted data with different mathematical models.

MTT assay outcomes were recorded after 24 h for L929 and U87-MG cells. A significant difference between the sample and control groups is indicated by ** with a p-value of less than 0.01 and a substantial difference with a p-value of less than 0.001, according to statistical analysis.
A comparison between the QC drug loading percentage and the half-life and effectiveness of encapsulation for various delivery methods.

row.entry: Platforms for the delivery of QC

head.ce:title: Formulation of double nanoemulsions based on pH-sensitive carboxymethyl cellulose /Starch copper doped carbon quantum dots for quercetin controlled release

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Natural Science, Yeungnam University, 280 Daehak‐Ro, Gyeongsan 38541, Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Natural Science, Yeungnam University, 280 Daehak‐Ro, Gyeongsan 38541, Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanocomposite Hydrogels are versatile and adaptable in drug delivery, suitable for various administrations. They offer prolonged and regulated drug release which makes them valuable in targeting cancer treatments. This study used the water/oil/water approach to construct a drug nanocarrier. It encapsulated hazelnut oil, serving as a protective and organic membrane that enveloped the nanocarrier and regulated drug release. Quercetin (QC), a potent flavonoid with potential therapeutic applications, faces challenges due to its hydrophobic nature and poor solubility. This approach presents a novel approach to enhance QC's solubility and stability by integrating it with copper carbon quantum dots (Cu-CQDs) into a pH-sensitive carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and starch-based hydrogel nanocomposite. This system aims to improve QC delivery while minimizing adverse effects. The nanocomposite exhibited exceptional drug loading and encapsulation efficiencies of 47.00 % ± 0.45 and 86.25 % ± 0.75, respectively, ranking among the top-reported values for similar systems. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was used to examine molecular interactions. X-ray diffraction (XRD) assessment demonstrated the presence of components beneficial for drug solubility enhancement at 2Θ = 17–24°, where QC's crystalline peaks are absent from the CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC XRD pattern. A particle size of 151.57 ± 37 nm is optimized for selectively targeting cancer cells, minimizing interaction with healthy cells, as revealed through DLS assessment. Morphological characteristics analyzed by FE-SEM support this observation as well. Zeta potential measurement (+38.8 mV) highlights nanocarrier stability. CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC displayed remarkable cytotoxicity against cancerous cells, reducing U87-MG cell viability to 48 % while maintaining high cell viability (92 %) for normal L929 cells. These results emphasize the promise of this nanocomposite as a QC drug delivery system by indicating improved anticancer efficacy with fewer adverse effects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer, a leading cause of death globally, poses a significant barrier to increased life expectancy. Despite mortality rate declines in conditions like stroke and coronary heart disease, cancer's global burden escalates [1]. Particularly concerning are brain tumours, fatal cancers most common in children, and a substantial morbidity and mortality source. Several treatment strategies exist, yet few drugs have received clinical approval, underscoring the urgent need for more effective therapies[2]. Approaches vary from surgery to radiation and chemotherapy, each carrying its unique benefits and limitations. Malignant brain tumour incidence shows a peculiar trend, declining overall but paradoxically increasing among children and adolescents. Cancer's pathogenesis remains linked to cell mutation-driven proliferation and abnormal growth despite this complexity. The limited options for effective cancer treatment, especially brain tumours, further underscore the disease's grim reality. These cancers resist conventional and advanced therapies due to their unique properties, necessitating ongoing research into novel treatment modalities [3–5].

Nanoparticles (NPs), sized between 1 and 100 nm, enhance cancer drug delivery systems (DDS) through increased water dispersion, biocompatibility, and biodegradability[6]. They improve drug solubility, half-life, and bioavailability, enabling targeted drug delivery and fewer side effects [7–11]. The versatile nanocomposite CDs@mSiO2–PEG forms suitable DDS for targeting therapy and cell imaging [12]. Nanoparticle (NP) drug transport mechanisms differ; carrier-mediated systems restrict large molecules. However, with versatile surface functions, ultra-small carbon dots favor large drug molecule delivery by covalent conjugation. Surface-modified nanoparticles show promise for enhanced drug delivery across the blood–brain barrier and other cancer therapies [13–17]. Hydrogels are 3D networks of hydrophilic polymers with high water content, offering tunable properties and high biocompatibility[18]. Nanocomposite hydrogels are hydrated polymeric networks crosslinked with nanoparticles or nanostructures, offering significant potential in the biomedical sector[19]. They capitalize on the pH differences between healthy tumour cells to design a regulated drug delivery system. This helps improve cell uptake, hasten drug release, and maintain stability, reducing the risk of early drug leakage that could harm healthy tissues [20]. A pH-responsive carrier based on CMC and Fe3O4@ increased the efficacy of entrapment by 2 % and drug loading efficiency from 86.75 % to 88.50 %. It retained most payload in neutral conditions (pH 7/4), releasing more in acidic environments (pH 5/4) with the sustained-release pattern. They capitalize on the pH differences between healthy tumour cells to design a regulated drug delivery system. This helps improve cell uptake, hasten drug release, and maintain stability, reducing the risk of early drug leakage that could harm healthy tissues[21]. The notable antibacterial properties of copper nanoparticles (Cu-NPs) have attracted the attention of cancer researchers. Cu-NPs have demonstrated their ability to induce apoptosis and kill cells in various cancer cell lines, including A-375, U937, and HeLa, originating from human skin melanoma, lymphoma, and cervical cancer [22,23]. Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are small carbon clusters enriched with hydrogen, oxygen, and nitrogen, possessing notable water solubility and intense luminescence. Contrary to regular carbon's properties, their distinct structure and carboxyl edge-groups confer high biocompatibility and exceptional nanoscale characteristics[24–26]. A green synthesis route was explored to create Cu-CQDs for histidine sensing and arsenate detection in water [27].Cu-CQD enhancement provides better conditions for specific antigen detection by improving sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and reproducibility[28].

The water-soluble Cu-CDs were synthesized using a simple hydrothermal process, demonstrating blue luminescence and excellent fluorescence quantum yield (9.81 %). This could lead to innovative diagnosis strategies for diseases like cancer[29]. In light of growing environmental concerns, naturally occurring polymers are preferred over synthetic ones [30,77]. Carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), a cellulose derivative, is prized for its biocompatibility, chemical stability, and biodegradability. CMC's anionic nature allows for hydrophilicity, bioadhesiveness, pH-sensitivity, and controlled drug release through ionic interactions, structure modifications, and drug delivery[31]. A smart, pH-sensitive CMC/PEG/HNT nanocomposite carrying 5-FU was synthesized, enhancing drug release at acidic pH (87 %) and showing targeted cytotoxicity against cancer cells (48.5 % apoptosis) [32]. Further, CoFe2O4/GO-ADH-CMC bio-nanocomposite was developed as an anti-cancer drug. These nanocarriers showed high entrapment/loading efficiency and potentially safe carrier with curcumin's continuous and slow release [33].

There's been an increased focus on improving the solubility and bioavailability of newly developed drugs, such as Quercetin (QC), which, despite its benefits, struggles with absorption due to its hydrophobic nature and poor solubility [34–38]. QC is a major dietary flavonoid found in fruits and vegetables, potentially affecting memory impairment, diseases, and cancer[39,40]. Despite its low bioavailability, it exhibits neuroprotective actions due to its ability to efficiently cross the blood–brain barrier[32].

Starch, a non-toxic natural polymer found in many plants, is preferred for pharmaceutical applications due to its cost-effectiveness, ease of modification, and versatility[41]. It is used to create nanoparticles for drug delivery systems due to its cross-linkable properties, and it naturally breaks down into biologically acceptable compounds, eliminating the need for removal post-drug delivery[42,43].

This study synthesised a novel pH-sensitive CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs hydrogel nanocomposite using a double emulsion (W/O/W) method to control QC release [34]. The current study aims to integrate Cu-CQDs with CMC and starch to increase the stability and solubility of QC. As previously indicated, the combining of Cu and CQDs results in a unique nanoparticle with antimicrobial properties. The nanocarriers' shape and crystalline or amorphous structures were studied using XRD and FESEM, while the connections between the nanocarrier components were evaluated using FTIR analysis. Furthermore, the MTT experiment validated the CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC nanocomposite's cytotoxic effects on U-87 MG cells.

Starch (C6H10O5)n, Sodium carboxymethyl cellulose (molecular weight = 90,000), Acid acetic (99.5 %), Span 80 (molar mass = 428.60 g/mol), diammonium hydrogen citrate (C6H14N2O7, 99.0–102.0 % (alkalimetric)), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9 %), copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O, ≥99.0 %) that were purchased from Merck Company. (Darmstadt, Germany). Quercetin drug (>95 % (HPLC), DMEM/high glucose, Dialysis bag (12Kd and 7Kd cut off) and MTT powder (97.5 % (HPLC), Fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (PEN) streptomycin (STR), was also obtained from Sigma Aldrich Co (USA).

The hydrothermal method was used for the preparation of Cu-CQDs. Mix 2 g of diammonium hydrogen citrate (C6H14N2O7) as the carbon source and 1 g of CuCl2·2H2O as the dopant in 75 mL of distilled water. Heat the solution to 230 °C while vigorously stirring for 20 min. NaOH is used to bring the pH of the solution to 7.0 after allowing it to cool to room temperature. Filter the obtained solution using a dialysis bag (7Kd) to remove impurities. Cu-CQDs are present in the final solution at a final concentration of 40 mg/ml [44].

Initially, dissolve 0.4 g of powdered CMC in 20 mL of a 2 % w/v acetic acid solution. Stir the mixture with a magnetic stirrer at a moderate RPM to obtain a homogeneous hydrogel solution. Thoroughly mix the hydrogel with 0.2 g of starch powder, then treat it in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. Add 20 mg of Cu-CQDs nanoparticles to the mixture and stir until homogenous [45].

To prepare the final nanocomposite, add 200 μL of QC (1 mg/mL) dropwise to a 25 mL CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs solution, resulting in a 5 μg/mL final drug concentration. Obtain a homogenous hydrogel CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs including QC. Place the hydrophobic phase (9 mL of Hazelnut oil with 2 % v/v SPAN 80) on a stirrer. Prepare a 3 mL volume of CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC hydrogel using a syringe and slowly add it to the hydrophobic phase. Intensely mix the solution to achieve a spherical and uniform shape, reducing the nanocarrier size in the hydrophobic phase. After 10 min of stirring, slowly add 9 mL of distilled water (hydrophilic phase). Allow the emulsion to settle for 15 min, then collect the oil phase. Isolate the nanocarrier from the aqueous phase through centrifugation at 4500 rpm (2490 rcf) for 10 min. Finally, place the samples in a refrigerator until completely frozen at −20 °C and transform them into powder using a freeze dryer (FD-1C-80). [46].

Nanoparticle morphology inspectedusingvia field emission scanning electron microscopy that operating at 30 kV (TESCAN, MIRA III model, Czech Republic). To assess the size distribution and stability of the nanoparticles, zeta potential measurements (Horiba SZ-100) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis (Mastersizer 2000S; Malvern Instruments, UK) were used. Following the inclusion of each component, The crystalline structure of the nanocomposite was analyzed via X-ray diffraction (Panalytical X'Pert PRO XRD diffractometer with a range of 10° to 80°). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) on )Bruker Instruments model Aquinox 55( was utilized to evaluate the interactions among all of the components in each sample and to find multiple functional groups in the composite.

The technique to assess loading and encapsulation effectiveness closely resembles the strategy described in earlier studies[47]. We introduced 1 mL of ethyl acetate to 1 mL of CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC to evaluate drug loading and entrapment efficiencies and separated the ethyl acetate phase after vigorously shaking the mixture to homogeneity. The concentration of QC was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 370 nm (the maximum absorption wavelength of QC) after the organic phase was separated. We used Equations (1) and (2) to compute the loading and encapsulation efficiencies.
(1)
L o a d i n g
E f f i c i e n c y
%
=
T o t a l
Q C
q u a n t i t y
-
F r e e
Q C
q u a n t i t y

nanocarrier

(2)
E n t r a p m e n t
E f f i c i e n c y

T o t a l
Q C
q u a n t i t y

Q C
q u a n t i t y
Total
QC
quantity

The nanocarrier release has been examined using the dialysis method in two separate phosphate-buffered solutions (PBS) with varied pH values. The pH 7.4 solution represents healthy tissues, while the pH 5.4 solution represents cancerous tissues. We first filled a dialysis tube (12kD) with one milliliter of CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC solution to simulate the body's natural environment. Afterward, we immersed the bag in 15 mL of 20 % v/v ethanol and placed it in a 37 °C warm water container. At predetermined intervals, we changed 300 μL of the samples with an equivalent volume of PBS after 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 6, 12, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h. The UV–Vis spectrophotometer measured the amount of QC in the buffer, and a calibration curve was used to determine the cumulative drug release percentage. The release percentage of QC was computed using Equation (3).
(3)
R e l e a s e d
Q C
p e r c e n t a g e
=
QC
drug
Loaded
QC
× 100

The MTT test assessed the cytotoxic effect of the prepared formulations on the human glioma cell line (U-87 MG). We used the L929 cell line as non-cancerous cells to investigate the cytotoxic potential of our drug delivery method and evaluate potential negative consequences. The control group received no treatment and only cultured in DMEM. The cells were cultured at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 and a humid environment in a medium containing 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotics. U-87 MG cells were cultured for a 24-hour incubation period to achieve > 75 % confluency in cell–cell attachment. Cells were cultured in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well using a medium consisting of 10 % FBS and 1 % antibiotic mixture (PEN and STR). For 72 h, cultivated cells were exposed to the prepared materials, with untreated cells as the control group. Each well was filled with 100 μL of MTT reagent, and the plates were left to incubate at 37 °C for four hours. After washing with PBS solution, 200 μL of DMSO was added to dissolve the formazan crystals. Cell viability was measured using an ELISA microplate reader operating at a 570 nm wavelength. Equation (4) was used to determine the percentage of cell viability[47]:
(4)
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
Sampleabsorption
controlabsorption
× 100

This method was employed to investigate the chemical bonds within the functional groups of Starch, CMC, CMC/Starch, CQDs, Cu-CQDs, CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs and CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC (Fig. 1 ). The peaks at 1407
cm
- 1
and 1631
cm
- 1
in the instance of CMC were connected to the symmetric and antisymmetric vibration of the carboxyl group. Additionally, the peaknear 3000
cm
- 1
showed stretching of the oxygen-hydrogen bond. CH2 scissoring and OH bending vibration are responsible for the peaks near 1420
cm
- 1
and 1325
cm
- 1
, respectively [48,49]. The presence of peaks at 1150, 1081, 1016, and 927
cm
- 1
in Starch signifies CO bond stretching. The distinct peaks located at 1080
cm
- 1
and 1016
cm
- 1
are identifiers for the anhydroglucose ring O–C stretching[50]. In the CMC/Starch spectrum, the primary alcoholic band absence in starch suggests its involvement, with interactions occurring at this site between Starch and CMC molecules. The broadening band observed between 2900 and 3600
cm
- 1
is connected to the blend's notable OH group representation. These groups originate from CMC, and Starch. Furthermore, the presence of the COONa that shifted to 1639
cm
- 1
and C = O groups confirms the success of the blending process and the strong interaction between the OH groups of the two polysaccharides causes the O–H stretching band to widen and move at 3440
cm
- 1
[45,51,52].The composite hydrogel peaked at 1110
cm
- 1
, originating from the –CH–O–CH2 unit. The occurrence of a hydrogen bond was indicated by shifts in the absorption bands for N–H and O–H vibrations. The amino group of CDs and the carboxy group of CMC formed this connection by a prominent absorption band showing up at 1628
cm
- 1
[53,54]. All the characteristic peaks of CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs remained intact upon the introducing of QC into the carrier. A notable reduction in intensity for some peaks like 1642
cm
- 1
and 1082
cm
- 1
signifies QC and CMC/Starch interaction [55].

XRD analysis is a valuable technique for understanding crystal structures and Fig. 2 depicts the patterns for the samples. In neat CMC, the broad peak observed at 2Θ = 21.8° signifies the presence of an amorphous structure [56]. The crystalline peak at 2Θ = 17.4° and the wide peak at 20–26° on starch indicate semicrystalline [57]. XRD was conducted to investigate the crystalline properties of CQDs. It has a wide peak at 2Θ = 24.8°. Well-defined peaks were observed at 2θ values of 75/55, 56/65, and 45/55, corresponding to the crystal lattice planes of copper nanoparticles of Cu-CQDs. Additionally, peaks at 2θ = 27.60° and 31.95° are commonly associated with carbon material [58–60]. CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs exhibit an amorphous character, as seen by the large peak observed at 2Θ = 21°. According to prior research, QC presents strong peaks from 12° to 25°, confirming its crystalline nature[21]. The addition of QC shows successful incorporation within the nanocomposite, where the XRD pattern of CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC does not display any of QC's crystalline peaks, signifying its encapsulation within the nanocomposite's pores enhancing nanocarrier crystallinity. CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC displays a broad peak at 2Θ = 17–24°, confirming component presence. This alteration is advantageous for our drug delivery system as the non-crystalline form of the drug exhibits higher solubility compared to the crystalline state [61].

The morphological characteristics of the synthesized CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC were explored through FE-SEM analysis, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The images show spherical-shaped nuclei that have aggregated in a clustered manner. The particle distribution and sizes are nearly uniform and consistent.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis is an essential method used to determine the size distribution of particles. In Fig. 3d, we present the results of a DLS experiment conducted on CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC nanocarrier. This suggests the development of a protective Span 80-hazelnut oil layer and membrane around the nanocarrier, resulting in an enlargement of particle size. FE-SEM images support this observation as well. Furthermore, a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.21 was measured, indicating a relatively uniform distribution of particle sizes in the nanocarrier. This size range (151.57 ± 37 nm) is optimal for selectively targeting cancer cells, as larger particles can pass through their membranes while minimizing interaction with healthy cells [62].

Zeta potential was measured at pH = 7.4. The outcomes are visualized in Fig. 3c. Zeta potential measurements revealed an interfacial charge range between −17.4 mV and + 80.9 mV, with a mean value of + 38.8 mV that indicates excellent stability. As established in the literature, these interactions are primarily driven by electrostatic forces involving the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups of Cu-CQDs that hold the QC. These interactions are of paramount importance in determining the stability and behavior of these complexes, rendering them highly promising for drug delivery applications [62,63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading and encapsulation of drug determination
ce:section.ce:para: As was previously mentioned, the low solubility and hence insufficient bioavailability of QC pose a substantial obstacle to its medicinal effectiveness. Therefore, enhancing the entrapment efficiency and drug loading on the nanocomposite is crucial.

This study recorded the determined drug loading (47.00 ± 0.45 %) and entrapment efficiencies (86.25 ± 0.75 %). A comparison with previous investigations focusing on platforms for QC delivery (as shown in Table 1 ) highlights that the achieved drug loading and encapsulation efficiency percentage ranks among the top reported values for similar systems. The porous structure of nanoparticles based on gelatinized starch and CMC, which allows for the trapping of more drug molecules and results in a noticeably higher drug loading percentage, is responsible for the increased drug loading. Furthermore, a great deal of interaction between the drug and carrier is made possible by the significant surface area of Starch/CMC/Cu-CQDs nanoparticles.

In two separate pH environments, pH 5.4 and pH 7.4, the drug release pattern was investigated as shown in Fig. 4 . These pH levels were selected to replicate the typical pH found in tumor microenvironments and the normal pH of blood. As illustrated, the release pattern included a rapid phase from 30 min to 24 h, followed by a slower phase from 24 to 96 h. After the first 24 h, 66 % of the drug had been released in pH 5.4, while 65 % remained within the nanoparticle in pH 7.4. By 96 h, almost all of the drug had been released in the more acidic environment of pH 5.4, but 35 % was still contained within the nanoparticle at pH 7.4. The nanoparticles' sensitivity to pH was demonstrated by the drug's release being slower at pH 7.4 and increased at pH 5.4. This behavior suggests that the nanoparticle allows for more effective drug release within the tumor microenvironment, potentially reducing side effects and indicates the presence of pH-sensitive swelling of CMC and Starch that at a pH level of 5.4, the cumulative release rate of drugs can achieve 96.2 % [68]. This stability could result from hydrogen bonds establishing between the carboxylic acid groups in CMC and the hydroxyl groups in QC [69]. The pH responsiveness of CMC and starch is thought to cause the pH-sensitive drug release[45]. Driven by carboxyl groups, CMC is sensitive to pH. Temperature, ionic strength, crosslinking, pH, and structure affect swelling. When pH is higher than pKa, the negatively charged carboxylic units cause electrostatic repulsion, which results in hydrogel expansion. At higher pH values, the ionization of carboxylate units causes swelling, whereas deionization causes the matrix to compress at lower pH values. When the pH of the solution is lower than the polymeric pKa, deionization of carboxylate units causes the polymeric matrix to contract. Because of their adjustable pH sensitivity, CMC hydrogels are useful for applications needing controlled swelling and dimensional changes in response to pH variations. According to research, QC bonds to the surface of carbon dots through electrostatic interactions between the hydrogel's hydroxyl and ether groups and the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups of CQDs [63,69,70]. Research has provided evidence the existence of functional groups within CQDs, encompassing N–H, C-OH/C-O-C, and COOH [71,72]. Together with the ether oxygen in starch and CMC, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of CQDs can also form hydrogen bonds. In an acidic pH environment, the amine groups become protonated, causing them to repel each other, essentially disrupts the electrostatic interaction. This protonation also affects the carboxyl groups, leading to a change in the electrostatic interaction and plays a significant role in drug release [73].

Statistical analysis has been used to assess the release data collected from the dialysis procedure in normal conditions (pH 7.4) and the tumor environment (pH 5.4). The developed CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC nanocomposite release data were fitted to first-order Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic models to identify the major QC release mechanism. As previously stated, the release behavior of QC was analyzed using the curve fitting model; R-squared values were examined; the outcomes are shown in Fig. 5 and Table 2 .

The Korsmeyer-Peppas model had the highest R2 with n values in the range of 0.48–0.8, indicating a release mechanism mostly governed by diffusion[74]. The created nanocarrier structure and the component interactions are compatible with this release mechanism. The drug's physical and chemical interactions with the nanocomposite components protect the nanocarrier's integrity and keep it from dissolving. Consequently, diffusion is the main emphasis of the release profile, which is consistent with the previously reported data.

We used the MTT assay, a helpful method for determining cytotoxicity, to assess the impact the created nanocarriers had on the U87-MG brain cancer cell line. Furthermore, we compared their effectiveness in inhibiting tumour growth with free QC. We also conducted similar experiments on the L929 cell line to evaluate possible adverse effects. Both varieties of cells received treatment with Starch, CMC, CMC/Starch, CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs, and CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC, and free QC. The concentration used for each sample was 5 μg/mL. These experiments were done three times to verify the accuracy, and the data are shown as means with SEMs. The group that received no treatment (control) exhibited 100 % cell viability after 24 h, verifying the quality of the cell lines (Fig. 6 ).

In contrast, all samples, including the final CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC nanocarrier, displayed more significant cytotoxicity in U87-MG cells compared to L929 cells. While CMC showed no significant toxicity, CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs nanoparticles exhibited about 21 % cytotoxicity against cancerous cells. Loading QC on the CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDSs hydrogel further reduced U87-MG cell viability to 48 %, indicating an improvement. Notably, 92 % of L929 cells survived through the incubation of CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDs@QC nanocarrier. The cytotoxicity data suggest that the prepared nanocomposite exhibits superior antitumor activity with reduced side effects [75,76].

The majority of commonly used QC delivery systems frequently have unfavourable consequences and have limited ability to load drugs. To increase the efficiency of QC against brain cancer cells (U87-MG), we created a QC nanocarrier in this study utilizing a water-in-oil-in-water emulsion approach. Our thorough characterizations demonstrated that these nanocarriers were successfully produced and that they show a strong connection with one another. Impressively, we achieved high QC entrapment and loading efficiencies, coming to 86.25 % and 47.00 %, respectively. Furthermore, our nanocarriers exhibited sustained and pH-sensitive drug release profiles, releasing 65 % and 35 % of quercetin at pH 5.4 and 7.4, respectively, within 24 h. Our MTT test results underscore that CMC/Starch/Cu-CQDS@QC nanocarriers induced approximately 52 % cell death in U87-MG brain cancer cells. Nevertheless, further in-vivo animal experiments are essential to evaluate potential internal drug toxicity before moving towards clinical trials. The promise of these compelling pH-sensitive nanocarrier for regulated delivery of anticancer medicines is, in short, firmly supported by our experimental evidence.

Mohammad Najafi: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Mehrab Pourmadadi: Investigation, Formal analysis. Majid Abdous: Writing – review & editing, Data curation, Supervision. Abbas Rahdar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Sadanand Pandey: Writing – review & editing, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Abbas Rahdar thanks from University of Zabol for funding (UOZ-GR-8906).
Happy NOWRUZ 1403, Persian New Year of Languages Around the World (1403/01/03, AmirMoez, Zahedan)
The authors (Sadanand Pandey) express profound gratitude to Shoolini University for their invaluable support, which played a pivotal role in facilitating the completion of work.
